text: Hello, my name is Dr. Robert Richard and what you're about to see is a relatively unedited video of a laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. If you'd like more information about this procedure or about any other procedures that we perform, you can contact us at our office or visit our website at thelongstreetclinic.com.
The surgeon introduces laparoscopic instruments from the patient's left side, while the assistant's instruments enter from the right, marking the beginning of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon elevates the colon to expose the small bowel, identifying the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon identifies the very first portion of the small bowel, visually confirming its location during the laparoscopic gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and begins the laparoscopic gastric bypass procedure by creating the first staple line on the small bowel.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to separate the small bowel, placing three rows of staples on each side and cutting down the center to create the first staple line.

The surgeon uses a stapling device to separate the small bowel, with the segment to the left of the staple line leading to the liver, while the segment to the right is prepared to be brought up to the stomach for the gastric bypass.
The surgeon performs the first transection of the small bowel using a stapling device, visibly separating the bowel into two sections.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the distal end of the omentum, which is the connective tissue located between sections of the small bowel.
The surgeon measures and marks a limb of small bowel, creating a segment 100 centimeters in length by measuring in five-centimeter increments.

The surgeon measures and marks a hundred centimeters distally along the small bowel, preparing to reattach the biliopancreatic limb on the left to the newly designated enteric limb on the right. An opening is created between these two limbs of bowel.
The surgeon uses suture material to hold the two limbs of small bowel together, then creates openings in the bowel using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the limb that connects to the bypass stomach, known as the biliopancreatic limb. This limb is prepared for attachment to the gastric pouch and the distal small bowel.
The surgeon uses a grasper to gently spread and increase the size of the openings in the small bowel, which were previously created using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to create a stapled anastomosis, visually attaching the biliopancreatic limb to the enteric limb of the bowel.

The surgeon uses a stapler to place three rows of staples on each side of the two bowel limbs, simultaneously cutting down the center to create an opening between the biliopancreatic limb and the enteric limb.
After firing the stapler, an opening is created, allowing bile and pancreatic enzymes to travel down the biliopancreatic limb and join the enteric limb of the bowel.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to close the common enterotomy, grasping the opening and firing the stapler to seal the connection between the biliopancreatic limb and the enteric limb.

The surgeon removes a segment of bowel, revealing the completed lower anastomosis for the gastric bypass, known as the jejuno-jejunostomy. This anastomosis visually connects the biliopancreatic limb to the enteric limb.
The surgeon reinforces the end of the staple line, known as the toe of the anastomosis, with a simple suture. Subsequently, the mesenteric defect is closed, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the mesenteric defect and begins closing it with permanent sutures in a running fashion to prevent future bowel obstructions.
The surgeon completes the lower portion of the anastomosis, known as the jejuno-jejunostomy, which connects the biliopancreatic limb to the enteric limb.
The surgeon prepares to bring the enteric limb of bowel into the upper abdomen by passing it behind the colon. The assistant raises the mesocolon to facilitate this maneuver.
The surgeon creates a small window through the mesocolon, visible in the upper left of the screen, which is the drape of tissue supplying blood to the colon.
The surgeon creates a window through the mesocolon, allowing the small bowel to be passed under the colon to reach the upper abdomen with minimal distance.
I do believe that this shorter distance allows us to provide less tension on the anastomosis, which, in my opinion, tends to have less of a risk of an anastomotic leak.
The surgeon navigates into the space behind the stomach, visible through the lesser sac, while maneuvering behind the colon.
The surgeon places the small bowel through the transverse mesocolon, positioning it into the upper abdomen. The focus then shifts to the upper abdominal area for further surgical steps.
The surgeon uses a grasper and a retractor inserted through the same incision to elevate the left lobe of the liver, providing clear access to the stomach in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon begins the upper part of the gastric bypass procedure by removing the gastroesophageal fat pad, providing enhanced exposure to the upper portion of the stomach where the esophagus enters, facilitating control over the size of the gastric pouch to be created.
The surgeon opens the space behind the stomach and begins to take down the small gastroesophageal fat pad, providing exposure to the upper portion of the stomach.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the fat pad, using a harmonic scalpel to cauterize and cut through the large blood vessels visible along the pad.
The surgeon achieves good exposure of the apex of the stomach, ensuring the creation of a small gastric pouch while avoiding a large posterior fundus.
The surgeon removes a piece of excess fat to clear the target space for the gastric bypass. The lesser curve of the stomach is identified as the site for creating a small gastric pouch.
The surgeon enters the space on the lesser curve of the stomach using a harmonic scalpel, creating a small window. Subsequently, a gastric pouch is formed using a stapling device.

The surgeon creates a horizontal staple line on the lesser curve of the stomach using a stapling device, placing three rows of staples on either side and cutting between them to form the gastric pouch.

The surgeon creates a horizontal staple line on the stomach and begins forming a gastric pouch by vertically stapling alongside a bougie passed down the esophagus, which helps determine the pouch's width.
The bougie is visible as it passes into the newly created gastric pouch, providing a guide for the stapler to ensure precise stapling without crossing the proximal stomach or esophagus.

The surgeon identifies remaining posterior tissue and carefully maneuvers to ensure the creation of a narrow gastric pouch. The window is created and attached to the space above, following the removal of the fat pad.
The surgeon passes the stapler into the space created by removing the fat pad, forming a narrow gastric pouch without leaving any retained fundus.
There is minor oozing observed at the staple line, which is allowed to stop naturally unless it becomes aggressive. The surgeon proceeds to identify the small bowel.
The surgeon enters the space behind the stomach, known as the lesser sac, by opening a thin layer of tissue to locate the small bowel that was previously passed from below.

The surgeon opens the space behind the stomach, controlling minor bleeding from the short gastric vessels. The limb of small bowel is brought into the upper abdomen, positioned in front of the residual stomach, and attached to the narrow gastric pouch to create a gastrojejunostomy anastomosis.
The surgeon places a stay suture at the lower portion of the stomach, preparing the area for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon sutures the first layer of the gastric pouch, using techniques to control the pouch's position during the suturing process.

The surgeon demonstrates how the small bowel layer easily reaches the gastric pouch without tension. The small bowel is sewn initially to the staple line and then behind it, ensuring a vascular gastric anastomosis.

The surgeon uses an endo stitch to perform a running suture on the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy, prioritizing speed and ease. Regular sutures are prepared for the inner layers to achieve precise suture placement.
The surgeon transitions to sewing behind the staple line to remove it, creating the posterior layer of the two-layered anastomosis for the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the anastomosis and uses a harmonic scalpel to remove the staple line, creating a gastrotomy into the gastric pouch.
The surgeon uses the active blade of the stapling device to open the gastric pouch, creating a visible window, and proceeds to remove the staple line from the anterior aspect.
The surgeon creates an opening in the gastric pouch, revealing the bougie inside the stomach. The harmonic scalpel is then used to create an opening in the adjacent small bowel.
The surgeon identifies an area on the small bowel and uses the active blade of the harmonic scalpel to create an enterotomy, providing access to the bowel lumen.
The surgeon begins the inner layer of the anastomosis by sewing the posterior layer in a running fashion using a simple vicryl suture, ensuring the opening size is satisfactory.
The surgeon attaches the inner layer of the gastric pouch to the apex of the small bowel at the opening, forming the posterior inner layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon ties the suture securely at the end and begins a running posterior layer of the anastomosis, stitching from the inside of the small bowel to the inside of the gastric pouch, progressing from one end to the other of the posterior layer.
The assistant holds the gastric pouch and small bowel in a stable position, facilitating the surgeon's rapid and precise placement of sutures in the anastomosis site.
The surgeon concludes the inner layer of the anastomosis by pulling the suture from inside the stomach to the outside, preparing to suture the outer layer of the gastric pouch.
The surgeon performs a similar suturing maneuver on the small bowel, navigating around the corner of the anastomosis, which visually appears flat but is circular in nature.

The surgeon completes the posterior inner layer of the anastomosis with the suture now positioned on the outside. An additional suture is introduced to replicate the suture layer on the anterior side, finalizing the inner layer of the anastomosis.

The surgeon begins suturing at the apex using a simple two ovicral suture, anchoring it to start the anterior layer from the outside of the small bowel, moving inside, and then proceeding to the outside of the gastric pouch.

The surgeon continues the suture line along the gastric pouch, ensuring the opening remains unobstructed. A sizing tube or bougie is prepared to be passed through the opening to maintain the desired size.
The surgeon passes a sizing tube, or bougie, through the opening in the gastric pouch, allowing control over the size of the anastomosis. The anesthetist carefully advances the bougie downwards.
The surgeon uses a 34 French bougie to ensure the creation of a specific-sized opening by tightly securing the suture around the bougie within the gastric pouch.
The surgeon continues the anterior suture line towards the posterior suture line, preparing to tie the two together to complete the inner layer of the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anterior suture line and proceeds to sew it to the posterior suture line, finalizing the inner layer of the anastomosis between the gastric pouch and the small bowel.
The surgeon begins creating the final anterior outer layer of the anastomosis using a simple running suture technique, completing the layered closure.
The surgeon is completing the anterior outer layer of the hand-sewn anastomosis, suturing it to the initial placating suture to finalize the two-layered structure.
The surgeon completes the two-layered hand-sewn anastomosis and removes the 34 French bougie from the surgical site.
The surgeon places a bowel clamp to gently compress the small bowel, followed by performing an upper endoscopy to test the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon moves to the head of the table and passes a flexible endoscope down the esophagus, while the assistant covers the surgical area with saline.

The surgeon uses a flexible endoscope to distend the gastric pouch and small bowel with air, visibly illuminating the esophagus. The scope is passed across the anastomosis, stretching the area under tension to check for air leaks or bleeding, confirming the patency of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs suction on the small bowel and removes the endoscope, concluding the air leak test of the anastomosis.

The surgeon returns to the operative table and prepares to close the mesenteric defect created during the procedure. The small bowel, positioned behind the colon and in front of the stomach, is secured with a permanent suture in a running, locking fashion to prevent bowel obstruction while maintaining its function.
The surgeon completes the repair of the transverse mesocolon defect, ensuring closure of the mesenteric defect with sutures.
The surgeon inspects the mesenteric defect from above and applies fibrin sealant circumferentially around the anastomosis to enhance sealing.
The surgeon identifies the omentum to cover the surgical area, removes the liver retractor, and concludes the laparoscopic gastric bypass procedure.
